title_en: Are Humanoid Robots Ready to Be Deployed?
title_zh: 人形机器人是否已具备部署条件？

paragraphs.en: On a recent sunny day in Silicon Valley, I visited the industrial headquarters of 1X Technologies. Security was tight, so I had to put a sticker over my cellphone’s camera and talk my way out of signing an N.D.A. before I was brought into an enormous space to meet Neo, the company’s home robot.
paragraphs.zh: 在硅谷最近一个阳光明媚的日子里，我参观了1X Technologies的工业总部。安保很严格，我不得不先把手机摄像头贴上遮挡贴膜，并且在被要求签署N.D.A.（保密协议）前，先想办法把话说开，随后才被带进一片巨大的空间去见公司研发的家用机器人——Neo。

paragraphs.en: Neo stands five feet six and has no facial features except for two black cameras in place of eyes. The robot is a humanoid—its design is inspired by the human form—and its proportions are a blend of those of the median American male and those of the median American female. But Neo has no skin. Instead, it wears a beige nylon turtleneck bodysuit, gloves, and padded shoes over a see-through carapace. Under that is a skeleton made up of more than a hundred whizzing motors and cordlike artificial tendons that control Neo’s limbs. Neo’s cozy, minimalist aesthetic allows it to blend into the background. If it served me an espresso at a café, I’m not certain I would look up from my phone.
paragraphs.zh: Neo身高五英尺六英寸，除了两只黑色摄像头代替的“眼睛”外，没有任何面部特征。它是一台类人机器人——外形设计受人体启发——其比例介于美国男性和美国女性的平均身材之间。但Neo没有皮肤，而是穿着米色的尼龙高领连体服、手套和带鞋垫的鞋子，外层覆盖在透明的甲壳之上。其内层是由一百多组高速运转的马达和类似细绳状的人造肌腱组成的骨架，控制着Neo的四肢。Neo温和而极简的外观让它能够自然融入环境。如果它在咖啡馆给我端来一杯浓缩咖啡，我不敢说自己一定会抬头看它。

paragraphs.en: The robot weighs just sixty-six pounds, and I was able to pick it up in a bridal carry. It communicates through a speaker in its chest, using several different voices; the default one is in a calm but authoritative masculine register, an A.I.-modulated mixture of several voice actors. Neo can talk, listen, and respond to commands. In its skull is a squarish motherboard, roughly the size of a slice of bread and studded with white capacitors that look like teeth. This is Neo’s brain. Using A.I., it tries to translate your words into physical actions. If you ask Neo to pick up a cup from a table, Neo will attempt to pick up the cup. If you ask Neo to put the milk in the refrigerator, Neo will attempt to put the milk in the refrigerator. But Neo doesn’t always succeed at these tasks, and no one really knows what it can or cannot do.
paragraphs.zh: 这台机器人仅重66磅，我甚至可以用“新娘抱”方式把它抱起来。它通过胸口的扬声器进行交流，能使用几种不同的声音；默认语音是一种平静但权威的男性音色，由多位配音演员经AI混合调制而成。Neo可以说话、听懂并回应命令。它的“头部”里有一块近似方形的主板，大小大约是一片面包片，表面嵌着像牙齿一样的白色电容器。这就是Neo的大脑。借助AI，它会尝试把你的语言指令转化为物理动作。比如你让Neo从桌上拿起一只杯子，它会尝试去拿；你要它把牛奶放进冰箱，它会尝试去放进冰箱。但是Neo并不总是能完成这些任务，也没有人真正清楚它到底能做什么、又不能做什么。

paragraphs.en: The robot is the creation of Bernt Børnich, the C.E.O. of 1X Technologies. Børnich, a forty-two-year-old Norwegian, has been obsessed with robots since he was a child. His firm used to be called Halodi, and it sold wheeled security robots from offices south of Oslo. But in 2022 Børnich relocated to Silicon Valley, changed the company’s name, grew out his hair, and started wearing designer streetwear. Neo was announced shortly afterward.
paragraphs.zh: 这台机器人由1X Technologies的首席执行官Bernt Børnich研发。42岁的挪威人Børnich从小就对机器人着迷。他的公司以前叫Halodi，曾在奥斯陆南部的办公室销售轮式安防机器人。但2022年，Børnich搬到了硅谷，改名公司，留起了头发，并开始穿起了设计师街头服饰。Neo不久后便正式发布。

paragraphs.en: Børnich believes in the supremacy of the human form. “We are incredibly well designed,” he has said. “I would argue this is kind of, like, why we won evolution.” The environment in which humanoids will operate is already built with able-bodied humans in mind. To open a door handle, you need a hand; to climb a flight of stairs, you need feet. Many roboticists are now working on humanoids, but Børnich takes biomimicry further than his peers: Neo uses tendons where other humanoids use motors, and Neo’s motherboard is in its head, while most humanoids have it stored in the chest. Børnich says humanoids have the potential “to do almost anything.”
paragraphs.zh: 博尼奇坚信人类形态的优越性。他说：“我们的人类身体设计得非常出色。我会说，这在某种程度上就是我们在进化中胜出的原因。”人形机器人将要工作的环境，本身就按完整人类来设计。要拧开门把手，你需要一只手；要上几级楼梯，你需要双脚。如今许多机器人研究者都在开发人形机器人，但博尼奇把生物仿生推得比同行更远：Neo 使用的是肌腱结构，而不是其他人形机器人常用的电机；Neo 的主板在头部，而大多数人形机器人的主板都装在胸腔。博尼奇认为，人形机器人有潜力“几乎做任何事”。

paragraphs.en: Mention Neo—or Børnich—to other roboticists and you will be met with a slight pause. Børnich’s technical foundation is solid, but many people feel that he’s getting ahead of himself. Compared with the A.I. that generates language and images, the “physical” A.I. that runs a robot remains underdeveloped. “The world still doesn’t have a ChatGPT equivalent for a robot,” Deepu Talla, who oversees robotics at the microchip designer Nvidia, told me.
paragraphs.zh: 你若向其他机器人专家提到 Neo 或博尼奇，通常会引来一瞬间的停顿。博尼奇的技术基础很扎实，但很多人觉得他有些走在前面。与生成语言和图像的人工智能相比，驱动机器人的“物理”人工智能仍然不够成熟。英伟达（Nvidia）这家芯片设计公司的机器人负责人 Deepu Talla 对我说：“目前的世界还没有一款相当于 ChatGPT 的机器人版本。”

paragraphs.en: “I think home comes later,” Carolina Parada, the head of robotics at Google DeepMind, said. “I don’t think it’s a lack of interest, or even capability.” Rather, she explained, it was a question of safety. Jeff Cardenas, the C.E.O. of the industrial-humanoid manufacturer Apptronik, told me, “Around small pets, around small children, there’s still work to be done.”
paragraphs.zh: 谷歌 DeepMind 的机器人负责人 Carolina Parada 说：“我认为家庭环境是后面的阶段。”她表示：“我并不认为这主要是出于兴趣不足，甚至能力不足。”相反，她认为这是安全问题。工业人形机器人制造商 Apptronik 的首席执行官 Jeff Cardenas 对我说：“在幼小宠物和小孩附近，仍然有很多工作要做。”

paragraphs.en: Børnich agrees that Neo should not be used around small children. Still, he is keen to test consumers’ tolerance for what he calls “robotics slop”: tasks that are clumsily executed. For example, in a demonstration for the Wall Street Journal in October, Neo took longer than a minute to retrieve a bottle of water. Although Neo has a list price of twenty thousand dollars, more than ten thousand customers have already placed a deposit for one.
paragraphs.zh: 博尼奇同意 Neo 不应该在小孩附近使用。不过，他很想测试消费者对他称之为“机器人糙作业”的容忍度——也就是动作笨拙、执行不够优雅的任务。例如，10 月份在《华尔街日报》的一次演示中，Neo 花了超过一分钟才取来一瓶水。虽然 Neo 的标价是两万美元，但已有超过一万名客户先行支付了订金。

paragraphs.en: The customers are who you’d expect—wealthy early adopters from the Bay Area, Los Angeles, and New York City. Will DePue, a former OpenAI employee, lives in San Francisco with tech-enthusiast roommates. “I think our house has three orders on humanoid robots,” he told me. “This is kind of like the new iPhone.” Sometime later this year, workers at 1X’s factory, near Oakland, will pack a Neo into a container that looks like an oversized AirPods case, load it into the back of a truck, and deliver it to DePue’s residence. “Neo will probably not be a perfectly polished product, but at least it’s, like, an early version of what’s to come,” DePue said.
paragraphs.zh: 客户也正是你会想到的那类人——来自旧金山湾区、洛杉矶和纽约市的富裕早期采用者。前 OpenAI 员工威尔·德普（Will DePue）与一群科技发烧友室友住在旧金山。他告诉我：“我觉得我们家里已经下了三台人形机器人的订单。”他补充道：“这有点像新款 iPhone。”今年晚些时候，位于奥克兰附近的 1X 工厂的工人会把一台 Neo 装进一个看起来像超大版 AirPods 盒子的容器，装上卡车后备箱，然后送到德普的住所。“Neo 可能不会是一个完全打磨过的产品，但至少它是一个‘将来之物’的早期版本。”德普说。

paragraphs.en: At 1X’s headquarters, my guide was Dar Sleeper, the head of product and design and Børnich’s informal second-in-command. Sleeper, who is twenty-seven, has already had a memorable career. After college, he joined the fashion brand Yeezy, then moved on to industrial design at Tesla. In other words, his first boss was Kanye West, and his second was Elon Musk. Børnich reminded him of Musk, Sleeper told me, if you “graft in some human empathy.”
paragraphs.zh: 在 1X 的总部，我的向导是 Dar Sleeper，兼任产品与设计负责人，也是 Børnich 的“非正式副手”。斯劳珀现在二十七岁，职业经历已经很亮眼。大学毕业后，他先加入了时装品牌 Yeezy，后来又转去特斯拉的工业设计部门。换句话说，他的第一任老板是坎耶·韦斯特，第二任老板是埃隆·马斯克。Børnich 曾说，斯劳珀让我想起了马斯克，只要你“再植入一点人文共情”。

paragraphs.en: In 2024, Børnich asked Sleeper to design a robot that didn’t scare children. Sleeper’s first attempt failed. “Basically, anyone under the age of a hundred was scared of it,” he said. (It had an eerily smooth black face.) His next iteration was better, but it still frightened children under the age of twelve. He added and removed facial features, experimented with skin textures, and went through hundreds of configurations of eye sockets. He settled, finally, on stripping as many features from Neo as he could, then covering it with soft textiles. “Even children under five seem to like it,” Sleeper said. “Babies are still scared, though.”
paragraphs.zh: 2024 年，Børnich 让斯劳珀去设计一台不吓坏孩子的人形机器人。斯劳珀第一次尝试失败了。“基本上，凡是没到一百岁的孩子都被它吓到了。”他回忆道。（它有一张诡异得很、过分光滑的黑色脸。）他后来的版本更好了，但仍然会吓到十二岁以下的孩子。他增减了面部特征，反复试验不同皮肤材质，并尝试了上百种眼窝配置。最终他决定尽可能把 Neo 的特征都去掉，再用柔软的织物包覆。“连五岁以下的孩子似乎也都喜欢它，”斯劳珀说，“不过，婴儿还是会害怕。”

paragraphs.en: Sleeper led me through a labyrinthine industrial space at the headquarters. About eight hundred people work there, in roles involving assembly, testing, and research and development. We passed lathes, metal mills, and racks of 3-D printers, all in continuous operation. We passed a safety-testing zone, where technicians in goggles instructed robotic hands to karate-chop ripe cantaloupes. We passed Neo’s staging environments, which included a bedroom, a bathroom, a kitchen, and a dojo with tatami mats on the floor. We stopped at a motion-capture studio, where, off to one side, two dozen decommissioned models knelt together, as if in prayer.
paragraphs.zh: 斯劳珀带我穿过总部那座迷宫般的工业空间。这里有大约八百名员工，从装配、测试到研发等岗位。我们经过车床、铣床和一排又一排持续运转的 3D 打印机。我们经过一个安全测试区，戴着护目镜的技术员在里面指挥机械手臂去劈开熟透的哈密瓜。我们又经过 Neo 的情景模拟区，里面有卧室、浴室、厨房，还有一间铺着榻榻米的道场。最后我们停在一个动作捕捉摄影棚，侧边摆着二十多个退役模型，跪在一起，像是在祈祷。

paragraphs.en: “Yeah, I see it. I’m on my way as we speak.”
paragraphs.zh: “我看到了。我现在就要过去。”

paragraphs.en: Cartoon by Ellis Rosen
paragraphs.zh: 埃利斯·罗森（Ellis Rosen）绘制

paragraphs.en: This was where Sleeper had taught Neo how to walk. He told me that he had used his own body as the reference for Neo’s gait. (Sleeper stands well over six feet tall, with Vitruvian proportions.) Referring to his engineering co-workers, Sleeper said, “A lot of these guys walk like this,” then pantomimed a hunched scurry, his hands clutching imaginary backpack straps. “But I was an athlete in college,” he continued. “I actually had an anatomically perfect run.”
paragraphs.zh: 这就是 Sleeper 教 Neo 走路的地方。他告诉我，他把自己的身体当作 Neo 步态的参照。 （Sleeper 的身高远超六英尺，体型符合维特鲁威比例。）他提到自己的工程同事时说：“这些人里有很多人就是这样走路。”接着他摆出弓着肩膀、急匆匆跑过去的姿势，两只手像是在抓着一条虚拟的背包肩带。“但我在大学时是一名运动员，”他继续道，“我的跑姿其实是解剖学上标准的。”

paragraphs.en: Sleeper puts the “bro” in tech bro. He played lacrosse at the University of Michigan, and, despite the controversies surrounding Kanye West, describes him as “one of the best creative geniuses of all time.” At one point, we were discussing a portable battery pack that Sleeper is designing for Neo. “It’s actually a nontrivial problem,” he told me. I asked him if he had ever used the word “nontrivial” before moving to Silicon Valley. “Probably not,” he said, and laughed. “I didn’t say a lot of shit before I showed up here.”
paragraphs.zh: Sleeper 简直把“bro”两个字彻底发挥到了极致。他在密歇根大学打过拉克罗斯球，而且尽管围绕 Kanye West 的争议很多，他仍称他是“有史以来最伟大的创意天才之一”。有一次，我们在讨论 Sleeper 为 Neo 设计的一款便携电池组。他对我说：“这实际上是个不小的问题。”我问他在搬到硅谷之前是否用过 “nontrivial” 这个词。他说：“可能没有，”并笑了笑。“在我来到这里之前，我没怎么说过那么多废话。”

paragraphs.en: Sleeper has built a world around Neo. He oversees an in-house woodshop, which he uses to design sets, props, and furniture, all to mimic the environments that Neo will have to navigate. His plans for the 1X campus involve both a television studio and a forest. On the day of my tour, Sleeper wore a striking beige sweater, which he’d designed himself, in the style of Neo’s bodysuit.
paragraphs.zh: Sleeper 围绕 Neo 打造了一个完整的世界。他负责一个内部木工间，专门用来设计布景、道具和家具，都是为了模拟 Neo 未来必须穿梭的环境。 他为 1X 校园制定的方案里同时包含了一个电视演播室和一片森林。参观当天，Sleeper 穿着一件醒目的米色毛衣——这是他自己设计的——款式模仿了 Neo 的机体服。

paragraphs.en: I have been reporting on Silicon Valley for some while, which is another way of saying that I have been served a lifetime supply of bullshit. Before my visit to 1X, I had been ready to call bullshit on Neo, too. The company had posted some splashy videos on social media a few months earlier, then gone dark, and I suspected that Neo was just more overpromoted vaporware.
paragraphs.zh: 我在硅谷写稿有一阵子了，这也可以说是我已经喝到一整辈子的‘胡扯’。在去 1X 之前，我也已经准备好对 Neo 大声吐槽了。公司几个月前在社交媒体上放出过一些很有视觉冲击力的视频，随后就消失了很久，我怀疑 Neo 也不过是又一个被炒作过度、空有其名的“幻影产品”。

paragraphs.en: In fact, I was ready to call bullshit on the whole robotics industry. At the Las Vegas Consumer Electronics Show this January, there were numerous robots on display, from many manufacturers. They all seemed clunky and loud. They clanked when they walked. Sure, one robot landed a backflip—but then a piece of it broke off and flew toward the crowd. Nothing there seemed ready for consumer use.
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，我甚至已经准备好对整个机器人行业都泼冷水。就在今年一月的拉斯维加斯国际消费电子展上，许多厂商都展示了各自的机器人。它们看起来都很笨重，而且噪声很大，走路时会发出金属敲击声。确实，有一台机器人做了个后空翻，但随后它的一块零件掉了下来，朝人群飞去。那些设备都看起来离可供消费者使用还有很长的距离。

paragraphs.en: But Neo was different. Neo was elegant. Neo was soft. Above all, Neo was quiet. With its padded feet and innovative tendons, it hummed along at twenty-two decibels, about the same volume as leaves rustling in a breeze. I felt a deep and horrible emotion triggered inside me, one familiar to any American—consumer lust. I wanted Neo in my house. I wanted it instantly, badly, immediately.
paragraphs.zh: 但 Neo 不一样。Neo 很优雅。Neo 很柔软。最重要的是，Neo 很安静。它用有缓冲的脚掌和创新的肌腱设计，运转时的声音只有 22 分贝，和微风拂过树叶的沙沙声差不多。我突然感到一种很沉重、很难受的情绪，一种任何美国人都不陌生的情绪——消费欲望。我立刻、迫切地想把 Neo 带回家。

paragraphs.en: Sleeper took me to a demonstration kitchen, where Neo effortlessly slotted dishes into a wire rack. I was impressed by the robot’s dexterity and fluidity of motion. Then I noticed a tele-operator standing to the side, wearing a V.R. headset, holding controllers, and dictating Neo’s every move. The robot I was seeing was a marionette. 1X declined to show me a demonstration of the A.I. that will power Neo. Sleeper told me that previous versions of Neo had tended to fall over. 1X said that this problem had been resolved, but, when I asked Sleeper if the latest models always remained upright, he said, “To say it doesn’t fall is, like, a total stretch.” Still, when I asked if he believed 1X would meet its deadline for home delivery in 2026, he answered with no hesitation: “It’s a promise we’ve made to the world, and a promise we’ve gotta keep.”
paragraphs.zh: Sleeper 带我去看了一个演示厨房，Neo 轻松地把餐盘一只手一只手地放进金属丝网架上。我对这台机器人的灵巧性和动作流畅性印象深刻。随后我注意到一名远程操控员站在一旁，戴着 VR 头显、握着控制器，并且在指挥 Neo 的每一个动作。我看到的这台机器人其实是一个提线木偶。1X 拒绝让我看到将来驱动 Neo 的人工智能演示。Sleeper 告诉我，Neo 的早期版本有时会倒下。1X 说这个问题已经解决了，但当我问 Sleeper 最新型号是否始终保持直立时，他说：“说它不会倒下，这说法实在太勉强了。”不过，当我问他是否相信 1X 能在 2026 年按时实现家庭配送时，他毫不犹豫地回答： “这是我们对世界作出的承诺，而我们必须守住这个承诺。”

paragraphs.en: Neo is one of a dozen humanoid robots scheduled to hit the market in the next twelve months. Its rivals include Figure’s 03 Humanoid, Boston Dynamics’s Atlas, and Apptronik’s Apollo. In January, Musk announced that he was converting a portion of a Tesla automotive plant in Fremont, California, into a robot-production factory, and hoped eventually to make a million Optimus robots there annually. His ultimate objective is to have robots working on the production line, making more robots, which he has called an “infinite money glitch.”
paragraphs.zh: 未来十二个月内，Neo 将成为约一打类人机器人之一即将上市。它的竞争对手包括 Figure 的 03 Humanoid、波士顿动力的 Atlas 以及 Apptronik 的 Apollo。1 月份，马斯克宣布他正在将加州弗里蒙特一座特斯拉汽车工厂的部分区域改造成机器人生产工厂，并希望最终在这里每年生产一百万台 Optimus 机器人。他的终极目标是让机器人站在生产线上工作，生产更多机器人，这也正是他称之为“无限金钱漏洞”的模式。

paragraphs.en: In China, efforts are equally ambitious. Unitree, based in Hangzhou, shipped more than five thousand of its G1 humanoids last year, making the company one of the world’s leading suppliers. The G1 stands about four and a half feet tall and has a hole in its head where a face would go. Its motors are loud, it clomps when it walks—it basically has no charm at all. Starting at around fourteen thousand dollars, it is also one of the more affordable advanced robots on the market, and, because it can run open-source software, it has become a favorite of academic robotics researchers. The G1 has also gained popularity with home-robotics enthusiasts—during the recent N.B.A. Finals, a G1 in a Knicks jersey attended watch parties outside Madison Square Garden.
paragraphs.zh: 在中国，相关努力同样雄心勃勃。总部位于杭州的 Unitree 去年出货了超过五千台其 G1 类人机器人，使公司成为世界主要供应商之一。G1 身高约四英尺半，头部有一个本应是脸部的位置。它的电机声音很响，行走时会发出重重脚步声，几乎没有任何“可爱感”。起售价约为一万四千美元，它也是市场上较为实惠的高端机器人之一，而且由于可以运行开源软件，它已成为学术机器人研究者的热门选择。G1 也受到了家庭机器人爱好者的欢迎——在最近的 N.B.A. 总决赛期间，一台穿着尼克斯球衣的 G1 曾在麦迪逊广场花园外参加观赛派对。

paragraphs.en: I first encountered the G1 at the robotics lab of Aaron Ames, a professor of mechanical engineering at Caltech. Wearing a V.R. headset and holding controllers, I moved the robot back and forth a bit, then had it do some light calisthenics. It seemed shaky, almost drunk. I tried to make it clap its hands, but the G1 resisted. “Try it vertically,” a graduate student at the lab suggested. I did, and the robot pantomimed a delicate golf clap, without ever quite touching its hands together.
paragraphs.zh: 我第一次见到 G1，是在加州理工学院机械工程教授 Aaron Ames 的机器人实验室。当我戴上 VR 头显并握住控制器后，先把机器人前后轻轻移动了一下，随后让它做一些轻量化体操训练。它看起来有点摇晃，几乎像喝醉了一样。我试图让它拍手，但 G1 不配合。实验室的一名研究生建议我：“试着竖着来。”我照做后，机器人做了个轻巧的高尔夫式拍手动作，却始终没有让双手真正碰到一起。

paragraphs.en: Later, in his office, Ames spoke for more than ten minutes, at a cadence of roughly two hundred words per minute, delivering a comprehensive takedown of 1X’s efforts. Ames believes that, no matter how sophisticated the engineering is, reliable A.I. for autonomous robots is years away. “I don’t know how 1X is actually going to get away with it,” Ames said. “I would worry about the legal ramifications when one of those robots falls on a person.”
paragraphs.zh: 后来，在他的办公室里，Ames 讲话超过十分钟，语速大约每分钟两百个词，对 1X 的努力进行了全面的拆解。Ames 认为，不管工程技术多么先进，可靠的自主机器人人工智能还需要数年才能实现。Ames 说：“我不知道 1X 到底是怎么能把这件事做到不出问题的。”他补充道：“当这些机器人中的一台摔倒砸到人时，我会担心会带来怎样的法律后果。”

paragraphs.en: Many industrial roboticists I spoke with were suspicious of the humanoid push. They told me that there are no standardized benchmarks for progress, and that videos of remarkable feats were often culled from hundreds of takes. In March, I talked with Modar Alaoui, a venture capitalist who invests in humanoid startups. He enthused about Iron, a robot from the Chinese manufacturer Xpeng, whose catwalk-inspired strut is so realistic that exhibitors have removed panels from its body to prove that it’s not a human in a robot suit. “Because of Xpeng’s work, I tell people not to work on locomotion anymore,” Alaoui said. “Locomotion is solved.” The day after I spoke to Alaoui, an Iron robot at a public demonstration at a mall in Shenzhen suddenly seized and fell over—it looked like it was having a stroke. The robot, which weighs more than a hundred and fifty pounds, could not recover, and three human handlers had to drag it away.
paragraphs.zh: 我与许多工业机器人专家交谈时，都对类人机器人热潮表示怀疑。他们告诉我，目前没有统一的进展评估标准，而且许多看起来很惊人的视频，其实是从上百次试拍中筛选出来的。3 月份，我与投资类人机器人初创公司的风险投资人 Modar Alaoui 交流。他对来自中国制造商小鹏的机器人 Iron 情有独钟，Iron 的模特步态像真的人体走秀一样逼真，展会方甚至拆下它机身上的面板，以证明它并非穿着机器人外壳的人。Alaoui 说：“因为小鹏的这项工作，我会告诉别人别再做行走系统了。行走这个问题已经解决了。”我在 3 月跟 Alaoui 谈话的第二天，深圳一家商场的公开演示中，一台 Iron 机器人突然抽搐般僵住并倒下，看起来像是中风了。那台机器人重达一百五十磅以上，无法自行恢复，最后是三名人工操作人员将其拖离现场。

paragraphs.en: To celebrate Lunar New Year this February, Unitree staged a display of dozens of G1s doing a choreographed kung-fu routine. The performance was real but misleading: the robots were executing a preprogrammed script, most likely derived from true wushu masters in motion-capture suits. And robotic martial artists can be dangerous to bystanders, as was recently demonstrated at a Chinese amusement park, where a G1 wearing a clown wig kicked a small child in the stomach. Outside of programmed scripts, robots struggle to perform autonomous actions in uncontrolled environments. “The same robot that can land a backflip might not be able to walk up a flight of stairs,” Parada, from Google, told me.
paragraphs.zh: 今年春节期间，Unitree 举办了一场表演，展示了数十台 G1 机器人执行编排好的功夫动作。这场演出是真实发生的，但具有误导性：机器人是在执行预设脚本，脚本很可能基于真正的武术大师在动作捕捉服中的动作录制。机器人武术表演者对围观者同样可能构成危险，这一点最近在中国一家游乐园也有展示：一台戴着小丑假发的 G1 曾踢中了一个小孩的腹部。脱离预设脚本之后，机器人在非受控环境中要完成自主动作就会很吃力。谷歌的 Parada 告诉我：“同一台会做后空翻的机器人，可能连上楼梯都做不到。”

paragraphs.en: Accidents are not the only concern; Neo might also obey a malicious command. Imagine a young child asking Neo to bash its robotic head against the dining-room table. Guardrails installed in the A.I. would almost certainly prevent the robot from following the command, at least initially. But research shows that such guardrails can be circumvented. If the child were persistent enough (in the way that small children tend to be) and creative enough (in the way that small children tend to be) he or she might succeed in getting the robot to comply.
paragraphs.zh: 事故问题并不是唯一的担忧；Neo 也可能服从恶意命令。想象一个小孩子让 Neo 把它的机器人头部撞向餐桌。安装在人工智能中的安全护栏几乎可以肯定会先阻止它执行这条指令。但研究表明，这些护栏是可以被绕过的。如果这个孩子足够坚持（就像小孩子通常那样）并且足够有创意（也像小孩子通常那样），他或她可能最终让机器人照做。

paragraphs.en: Robots can also be hacked. Last year, the security researchers Andreas Makris and Kevin Finisterre found a vulnerability in Bluetooth that would enable them to take control of a fleet of Unitree G1s, infecting one after another and “creating a robot botnet that spreads without user intervention,” they wrote. Humanoid robots need cameras and microphones to make their way through the world, which creates privacy concerns. Plus, their actions are unpredictable. “Babysitting these robots demands an entire operation team,” Jim Fan, a research scientist at Nvidia, wrote at the end of last year. “Mistakes are irreversible and unforgiving.”
paragraphs.zh: 机器人也可能被黑。去年，安全研究员安德烈亚斯·马克里斯（Andreas Makris）和凯文·费尼斯特雷（Kevin Finisterre）发现了一个蓝牙漏洞，这使他们能够控制一批 Unitree G1 机器人，在其中一台感染后再感染下一台，并“创建一个无需用户干预就能自行扩散的机器人僵尸网络”，他们写道。类人机器人为了在世界中行走需要摄像头和麦克风，这引发了隐私担忧。此外，它们的行为也不可预测。英伟达（Nvidia）的一名研究科学家吉姆·范（Jim Fan）在去年年底写道：“照看这些机器人需要一个完整的运营团队。错误是不可逆的，而且不留情面。”

paragraphs.en: Still, Parada conceded, putting robots in the home is “the ultimate dream.” Growing up in Venezuela, she fantasized about having a robot do her chores. Several roboticists I spoke with seemed a little disappointed by the current state of the field, compared with the expectations they’d had as children—nothing inanimate one day just started moving, nothing ever magically came to life. Sleeper recalled moments as a child when, he said, he “looked at stuffed animals, and got bummed out about how fucking lame they were.” Technology, he told me, sometimes made him sad: “It’s all just going into a screen, instead of into the world.”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，Parada承认，把机器人带进家庭是“终极梦想”。她在委内瑞拉长大时，就曾幻想有一台机器人来帮她做家务。我采访过的几位机器人专家也对该领域当前的状态有些失望，与他们小时候的幻想相比差距很大——那时候他们以为某天有东西一动不动的就会自己活动起来，会有些什么神奇地“活过来”。Sleeper回忆起他童年时期的片段，说他当时“看着毛绒玩偶时，会对它们有一种无比失望的感觉，因为它们实在是太没意思了”。他对我说，科技有时会让他感到沮丧：它“只是被塞进了屏幕里，而不是进入了现实世界”。

paragraphs.en: After I left 1X’s offices, I took my first ride with the autonomous ride-sharing service Waymo. The Waymo was clean and quiet, and avoided a congested highway by navigating through side streets and frontage roads. But, after delivering me to my destination, the car turned in to a crowded In-N-Out Burger drive-through lane, which can take more than an hour to pass through. I laughed out loud, preparing to report on yet another epic A.I. fail—until the car executed a crisp three-point turn, escaped the line, and drove off.
paragraphs.zh: 离开1X办公室后，我第一次乘坐了 Waymo 的自动驾驶网约车服务。那辆 Waymo 车很干净、很安静，还通过穿行在支路和辅道上避开了拥堵的高速公路。但在把我送到目的地后，车辆却转进了一个拥挤的 In-N-Out Burger 车道，这段车道有时需要一个多小时才能通过。我忍不住大笑，准备报道又一次“史诗级”AI 失误——结果车辆果断完成了一个三点掉头，脱离了车道，驶了出去。

paragraphs.en: I later learned that, in uncertain situations like this, a Waymo will sometimes request assistance from a human operator. Professional tele-operation “pilots,” working from the Philippines, oversee galleries of vehicle-camera feeds, alert for construction zones and lines of hungry Californians. Such pilots are also at work in other industries: in Japan, 7-Elevens do “autonomous” stocking using robot arms, which can be controlled from afar by workers wearing V.R. headsets. Human pilots also provide training data for A.I., and Waymo has used at least half a million hours of real-world data to fuel its autonomous-driving model.
paragraphs.zh: 后来我了解到，在像这样的不确定情境下，Waymo 有时会向人工操作员寻求协助。来自菲律宾的专业“远程操控”飞行员会监控大量车辆摄像头画面，留意施工区域和一排排正在等待的加州饥饿食客。类似的飞行员也在其他行业工作：在日本，7-Eleven 使用由机器人手臂完成的“自动化”补货作业，工人佩戴 VR 头显进行远程操控。此外，人工飞行员还会为人工智能提供训练数据，而 Waymo 至少已经使用了五十万小时的真实世界数据来驱动其自动驾驶模型。

paragraphs.en: A car merely has to avoid hitting things. A robot has to manipulate them without breaking them. 1X previously emphasized tele-operation in its marketing, but this generated pushback from potential customers. Now the company emphasizes A.I., but it hasn’t abandoned tele-operation: part of 1X’s gamble is that its customers will come to terms with the fact that a human might be peering into their home through Neo’s eye cameras. (DePue, the early adopter in San Francisco, told me that he would feel safer with a stranger tele-operating his robot than with a stranger cleaning his home.) Tele-operators will sit next to the A.I. team at 1X’s campus in Silicon Valley, and when the lighted rings on Neo’s earpieces change color customers will know that it is being remotely operated. “If you want a robot to bartend your party, we’ll get one of our operators,” Børnich said. “It’s also useful data for us.”
paragraphs.zh: 汽车只要避免撞到东西就行；机器人则必须在不弄坏它们的前提下操作这些东西。1X 之前在宣传中强调了遥操作，但这引起了潜在客户的反对。如今公司强调人工智能，但并未抛弃遥操作：1X 的赌注之一是让客户接受这样的事实，即有可能有人通过 Neo 的眼部摄像头窥视他们的家。（在旧金山的早期用户 DePue 告诉我，他会更愿意由陌生人远程操控他的机器人，而不是由陌生人进到他家里打扫。）遥操作员会坐在加州硅谷 1X 校园的人工智能团队旁边，当 Neo 耳罩上的指示灯环变色时，客户就会知道机器人正在被远程操控。“如果你想要一个机器人给你聚会调酒，我们就会派一名操作员来做。”Børnich 说。“这对我们来说也很有价值。”

paragraphs.en: Data is to the A.I. revolution what coal was to the industrial one. A.I. communicates in discrete units of data called tokens. In language processing, a token might represent a few letters. In robotics, a token might represent a trajectory associated with a finger joint. But language models have the text of the entire open internet to learn from, not to mention the (uncompensated) copyrighted work of human authors, lifted from the dark web. There exists no equivalent repository—legal or otherwise—of motion trajectories for joints.
paragraphs.zh: 在人工智能革命中，数据就像工业革命中的煤炭一样关键。人工智能以被称为“token”的离散数据单元进行运算。在语言处理里，一个 token 可能代表几个字母；在机器人领域，一个 token 可能表示与某个手指关节相关的一段运动轨迹。但语言模型可以学习整片公开互联网的文本内容，更不用说那些来自暗网的、未经补偿的人类作者版权作品。到目前为止，并不存在用于关节运动轨迹的等效资料库——无论合法与否。

paragraphs.en: Cartoon by Edward Koren
paragraphs.zh: 爱德华·科伦（Edward Koren）漫画

paragraphs.en: One approach to this problem is to radically increase motion-capture efforts. The German robot developer Neura has put more than a thousand industrial workers in motion-capture suits, and is using these data to train humanoids. “Everyone is scared of a shitstorm, because it’s, like, ‘Oh, they’re replacing your work,’ ” David Reger, Neura’s C.E.O., told me. “It’s not! We’re getting, like, a lot of data.” This didn’t seem to address the job-loss question, so I pressed Reger on what would happen to the labor market if his efforts succeeded. He straightened his posture and insisted that, in the next few years, the U.S. would need these robots. “You will have a decrease of working labor, and then you will feel it,” he said. “Right now, you still don’t feel it, but in Europe, we feel it a lot. We think everyone disappeared somehow.”
paragraphs.zh: 解决这一问题的一种方法，是大幅扩大量捕捉（motion-capture）工作。德国机器人开发商 Neura 已经让一千多名工业工人穿上动作捕捉服，并用这些数据来训练类人机器人。Neura 首席执行官 David Reger 告诉我：“大家都在害怕一场‘风暴’，因为大家会说‘哦，他们要替代你的工作了。’”他又说：“不是这样的！我们正在获取大量数据。”这似乎没有正面回答就业流失问题，于是我追问如果他的努力成功了，劳动力市场会怎样变化。Reger 坐得更挺了一下，坚称在接下来的几年里，美国将需要这些机器人。“你会看到劳动力需求下降，而且你会切身体会到这一点，”他说。“现在你可能还没感觉到，但在欧洲，这种感觉非常明显。我们觉得大家都像是突然不见了。”

paragraphs.en: Even if the entire population of Earth donned motion-capture suits, it would take decades to generate the amount of data that was used to train ChatGPT. Another approach is to train robots using videos from head-mounted cameras which have been posted on YouTube. With a little engineering magic, these “egocentric” videos can be turned into joint-trajectory data sets. More advanced video models might be able to extrapolate data from third-person views of real-world experiences such as sports games and cooking shows.
paragraphs.zh: 即使让全世界所有人都穿上动作捕捉服，也要几十年才能生成用于训练 ChatGPT 的那种数据量。另一种思路是用 YouTube 上发布的头戴式摄像头视频来训练机器人。只要加上一些工程手段，这些“第一视角”视频就可以被转化为关节轨迹数据集。更先进的视频模型甚至可能从第三人称视角的真实世界画面中推断出数据，例如体育比赛和烹饪节目中的经历。

paragraphs.en: Some robotics companies don’t build hardware at all. Instead, they are focussed on helping existing robots navigate the physical world. About two and a half miles north of 1X’s headquarters, I visited the startup Skild AI. The two firms could not be more different. 1X’s robots are exquisite objects of high design. Skild’s lobby looked like an emergency room, with deactivated Unitree robots hanging from cranes and lying on tables. One was on a gurney; another sat in a wheelchair.
paragraphs.zh: 有些机器人公司根本不造硬件。相反，它们专注于帮助现有机器人在现实世界中导航。大约在 1X 总部以北两英里半处，我参观了初创公司 Skild AI。两家公司可以说截然不同。1X 的机器人是精致高设计的物件，而 Skild 的前厅更像一个急诊室：失效的 Unitree 机器人被吊在吊车上，躺在桌上。一个被放在担架上；另一个坐在轮椅上。

paragraphs.en: There, I met with Skild’s co-founders, Deepak Pathak and Abhinav Gupta, both professors at Carnegie Mellon University. If Sleeper and Børnich are confident marketers, Pathak and Gupta represent a different Silicon Valley archetype: the entrepreneur whose technological convictions lead him to ignore the pressure of public opinion. This was not the type of company that was going to plant a forest on campus.
paragraphs.zh: 在那里，我见到了 Skild 的联合创始人 Deepak Pathak 和 Abhinav Gupta，他们都是卡内基梅隆大学的教授。若说 Sleeper 和 Børnich 是很会说服人的市场营销型人物，那么 Pathak 和 Gupta 则代表了另一种硅谷范式：技术信念坚定到足以无视公众舆论压力的创业者。这绝不是那种会在校园里种树的公司。

paragraphs.en: Skild is attempting to build a “general purpose” physical A.I.: a single brain that can be slotted into any body. This brain should be able to control not just a humanoid but also a set of detached arms, a robot animal, or a wheeled cart. Pathak and Gupta have to stress-test these robots, so they spend a lot of time kicking them, punching them, and generally harassing them—at one point, they showed me a video of a man using a chainsaw to cut off the legs of a robotic dog. Within seconds, the dog began walking on the stumps.
paragraphs.zh: Skild 正在尝试打造一套“通用”物理人工智能：一个可以装入任何机体的单一“大脑”。这个“大脑”应当能够控制的不仅是类人机器人，还包括一组独立的机械臂、一台仿生机器人动物，或一辆有轮子的手推车。Pathak 和 Gupta 必须对这些机器人进行压力测试，所以他们会花大量时间踢它们、打它们，并且经常“折磨”它们——有一次，他们给我看了一段视频：一个人用电锯把一只机械狗的腿锯掉。几秒钟后，这条狗就开始用断肢在地上行走。

paragraphs.en: I did not initially understand why Skild was treating its robots this way. (Nor did I understand why the chainsaw video was scored to hair metal.) Pathak explained that the ability to compensate for injury is one of the most important aspects of generalized physical intelligence. In Skild’s workshop were dozens of partially assembled robots, from a variety of manufacturers; one was wandering around without a head. Midway through the tour, we stopped in front of a robotic dog. One of its eyes was disabled, and its wiring was exposed; an employee was kicking it repeatedly. I was invited to join in.
paragraphs.zh: 我起初不明白Skild为什么要这样对待它的机器人。（我也不明白为什么那段链锯视频配的是发型金属乐风。）Pathak解释说，能够在受伤后进行代偿是通用身体智能（generalized physical intelligence）最重要的组成部分之一。在Skild的车间里，摆放着数十台半成品机器人，来自不同制造商；其中有一台四处晃动着却没有头。参观进行到一半时，我们停在一只机器人狗前。一只眼睛失效了，内部线路暴露在外；一名员工正反复踢它。我被邀请加入其中。

paragraphs.en: “Kick it like how you would kick a human,” Pathak said.
paragraphs.zh: “就像你踢人时那样去踢它，”Pathak说。

paragraphs.en: “I wouldn’t kick a human,” I said.
paragraphs.zh: “我不会踢人。”我说。

paragraphs.en: “Don’t overthink it,” Pathak said.
paragraphs.zh: “别想太多，”Pathak说。

paragraphs.en: After a moment’s hesitation, I planted my heel on the robot dog’s body and shoved it with force.The dog barrel-rolled across the showroom floor but was upright within half a second of coming to a stop.
paragraphs.zh: 犹豫了一会儿后，我把脚跟踩在这只机器狗的身体上，狠狠地用力推了一下。机器人狗在展厅地面上翻了个筋斗式翻滚，停下后不到半秒就重新站了起来。

paragraphs.en: Later, in a conference room, Pathak and Gupta explained that their seemingly cavalier attitude toward robot welfare was actually motivated by concern for human safety. (Better that a human kick a robot a thousand times than that a robot kick a human once.) Right now, they don’t think that humanoids are safe enough to use in residential settings—in fact, Pathak refuses to have one in his home. “People are using appearance as a way to misguide the public,” Pathak said. “If you make a robot humanlike, you expect it to have humanlike capabilities. But technology is far behind that.’ ”
paragraphs.zh: 后来在会议室里，Pathak 和 Gupta 解释说，他们看似漫不经心的“机器人福利”态度，其实是出于对人类安全的担忧。（与其让机器人踢一次人，不如让一个人踢机器人一千次。）他们现在并不认为类人机器人在住宅环境中安全到足以使用——事实上，Pathak 拒绝在自己家里摆放一台。“人们正在用外形来误导公众，”Pathak 说，“如果你把一台机器人做得像人，就会让人认为它具备人类般的能力。可技术离那还差得很远。”

paragraphs.en: Skild is one of the best-funded robotics startups, but much of its capital is spent on buying computing power from data centers. This is another way of saying that most of its money eventually goes to Nvidia. Nvidia, which has a five-trillion-dollar market capitalization, is the most valuable company in the world. In fact, adjusting for inflation, Nvidia may be the most valuable company in human history; its rival for the title is the Dutch East India Company. Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s C.E.O., has cosmic ambitions. “Wherever the universe has structure, we can turn that into A.I.,” he recently said.
paragraphs.zh: Skild 是资金最充足的机器人创业公司之一，但其大部分资金都花在从数据中心购买算力上。换句话说，它的钱最后大都流向英伟达。英伟达市值高达 5 万亿美元，是世界上最有价值的公司。事实上，按通胀调整后，英伟达甚至可能是人类历史上最有价值的公司；与之竞争这个头衔的对手是荷兰东印度公司。英伟达首席执行官黄仁勋怀有“宇宙级”的宏愿。他最近说过：“只要宇宙中存在结构，我们就能把它转化为 AI。”

paragraphs.en: Nvidia moved into robotics in 2014. Around this time, Huang recalls, he watched a neural network teach a robotic arm to shoot a hockey puck; he immediately calculated a rough estimate of the eventual size of the robotics A.I. market. Soon, Nvidia was touting virtual-reality simulators to train robots, and microchips to deploy in robot brains. (The simulators allow developers to experiment with surface friction, mimic faulty joints, and even alter gravity.) These early initiatives struggled at first, but Nvidia was able to establish what a former employee called “vender lock,” making robotics manufacturers dependent on it. Every single robot I encountered while reporting this article ran on an Nvidia microchip, and every single one had trained in an Nvidia digital gym.
paragraphs.zh: 英伟达于 2014 年进入机器人领域。黄仁勋回忆说，那时他看见一个神经网络教一只机械臂把冰球击飞，于是他立刻粗略估算了未来机器人 AI 市场的规模。很快，英伟达就开始推广用于训练机器人的虚拟现实模拟器，以及用于安装在机器人“大脑”中的芯片。（这些模拟器让开发者可以试验表面摩擦、模拟关节故障，甚至改变重力。）这些早期项目起初进展不顺，但英伟达成功建立了前员工所说的“供应商锁定”效应，让机器人制造商对它产生依赖。整篇报道期间我遇到的每一台机器人都运行着英伟达芯片，而且每一台都在英伟达的数字训练馆里完成过训练。

paragraphs.en: Nvidia has also invested in many startups, including Figure, Neura, and Skild. This has produced accusations of “circular” dealmaking from skeptical investors, since Nvidia is providing funds to its own customers, who then use that money to purchase its hardware. Michael Burry, the investor made famous in “The Big Short” for betting against the housing market in the late two-thousands, today warns of “suspicious” revenues at many A.I. companies, including Nvidia. Huang has called such claims “ridiculous,” and defended the investments, saying that they are necessary to expand Nvidia’s “ecosystem.”
paragraphs.zh: 英伟达还投资了许多初创公司，包括 Figure、Neura 和 Skild。这引发了怀疑投资者对其“循环式”交易的指责，因为英伟达在给自己的客户公司提供资金，而这些客户随后又用这笔钱购买英伟达的硬件。投资人迈克尔·伯里（Michael Burry）在《大空头》中因2000年代末对房地产市场做空而成名，他如今警告称，包括英伟达在内的许多人工智能公司存在“可疑”的收入来源。黄仁勋称这些说法是“荒唐的”，并为这些投资辩护，称其是扩展英伟达“生态系统”所必需的。

paragraphs.en: Nvidia’s robotics microchips are different from the ones they sell for A.I. training. They are “edge” chips, meaning that they do their processing in the robot instead of in a remote data center. The human brain uses about twenty per cent of the energy that the body generates; an Nvidia edge chip can drain as much as sixty per cent of a robot’s electricity supply. “It’s not the motors that take up most of the battery,” Cardenas, the C.E.O. of Apptronik, told me. “It’s actually the compute.”
paragraphs.zh: 英伟达用于机器人领域的芯片与其出售的人工智能训练芯片不同。它们是“边缘”芯片，意思是计算在机器人本体内完成，而不是在远端数据中心。人类大脑只消耗人体约二成能量；而一块英伟达边缘芯片最多可耗掉一台机器人高达六成的电量。Apptronik 的首席执行官 Cardenas 告诉我：“消耗大头并不是电机，而是计算。”

paragraphs.en: But, if a robot needs more brainpower, it can draw it from a local server via Wi-Fi. A robot’s A.I., like most computer software, is regularly upgraded via the internet; robots get smarter every week. Most importantly, they have a hive mind. “Whatever one robot learns, the others all learn at the same time,” Børnich said.
paragraphs.zh: 但如果机器人需要更多算力，它可以通过 Wi-Fi 从本地服务器调取。机器人的人工智能，像大多数计算机软件一样，会定期通过互联网升级；机器人每周都会变得更聪明。更重要的是，它们拥有集群大脑。“只要一台机器人学会了什么，其他机器人也会同时学会，”Børnich 说道。

paragraphs.en: This means that the wider the release of humanoids, the more skilled they become. If a thousand humanoid robots are sent to fold towels, they’ll learn from one another’s mistakes, and will adapt far faster than a single robot would. This network effect may account for 1X’s eagerness to get robots in the home. “There is definitely a data flywheel there,” Børnich said.
paragraphs.zh: 这意味着，人形机器人的投放越广，它们就会变得越成熟。如果有一千台人形机器人被派去折叠毛巾，它们会从彼此的错误中互相学习，进步速度会远快于单台机器人。正是这种网络效应可能解释了 1X 想把机器人带进家庭的迫切心情。Børnich 说：“这里肯定存在一个数据飞轮效应。”

paragraphs.en: “Robot” is one of a small number of words that have entered the English language via Czech. (“Polka” and “howitzer” are others.) The term was first used in Karel Čapek’s play “Rossum’s Universal Robots,” from 1920. In the play, an industrial robotics conglomerate has transformed the world economy: robots do most of the work, and they are also deployed as soldiers. At the same time, the human birth rate declines to zero. Eventually, the robots take over, and kill almost everyone on Earth.
paragraphs.zh: “机器人”是极少数从捷克语进入英语的词之一（“波尔卡”和“榴弹炮”也是其中的例子）。这个词最早出现在卡雷尔·恰佩克（Karel Čapek）1920年的戏剧《罗瑟姆的万能机器人》里。戏剧中，一个工业机器人集团改变了世界经济：机器人完成了大多数工作，而且也被派去当士兵。与此同时，人类出生率下降为零。最终，机器人接管了世界，并几乎杀死了地球上所有人。

paragraphs.en: Any extended discussion of robots, I have found, will eventually reference the apocalypse. Even in more positive treatments, such as Isaac Asimov’s “I, Robot,” the androids end up taking over. When I broached the subject of the robot revolution with Børnich, I sensed a certain fatigue. “Look, they won’t seek revenge on us,” he said. “We can wipe their minds.”
paragraphs.zh: 我发现，一旦谈到机器人，讨论几乎都会最终指向世界末日。即使在更积极的作品中，比如艾萨克·阿西莫夫的《我，机器人》，那些类人机器人最终也会接管一切。当我向博尼希（Børnich）提起机器人革命这个话题时，我察觉到他有些疲倦。他说：“你看，他们不会向我们复仇。” “因为我们可以抹掉他们的大脑。”

paragraphs.en: Perhaps the fearful visions of Čapek—not to mention those of James Cameron, in his “Terminator” movies—have tainted the West’s cultural perception of robots. David Reger, Neura’s C.E.O., told me that American journalists ask him anxious questions about robots, but South Korean journalists just seem impatient. “They’re tired of waiting!” he said. “They ask, ‘When is it coming?’ ” Reger’s observation is supported by data. A Stanford University analysis of opinion polls across three dozen countries found that respondents in China, South Korea, Thailand, and Indonesia were the most excited about consumer applications of A.I. Respondents in the U.S. and Canada were the least.
paragraphs.zh: 也许恰佩克的恐怖想象——更不用说詹姆斯·卡梅隆在《终结者》电影中的想象——已经影响了西方社会对机器人的文化认知。Neura 的首席执行官戴维·雷杰（David Reger）告诉我，美国记者会对他提出充满焦虑的问题，但韩国记者则只是显得很不耐烦。他说：“他们都等得不耐烦了！” “他们会问：‘什么时候会到来？’” 雷杰的观察得到了数据支持。斯坦福大学对三十多个国家民意调查的分析发现，中国、韩国、泰国和印度尼西亚的受访者对人工智能的消费级应用最感兴奋，美国和加拿大的受访者最不感兴趣。

paragraphs.en: Most of the roboticists I spoke with seemed more excited by the industrial applications of humanoid robots than by the prospect of their doing domestic chores. (“You’ll see them in McDonald’s first,” Rev Lebaredian, who runs physical A.I. simulation at Nvidia, said.) Apptronik’s Apollo humanoid—porcelain white, with knobby limbs and thick, sturdy legs—has been used in production at European automotive factories owned by Mercedes-Benz. For now, the robot is limited to activities such as picking out parts and loading conveyors. It needs to be fairly strong to complete this task, which means that a malfunction might hurt someone. For this reason, the robots are fenced off from the rest of the workers.
paragraphs.zh: 我采访的大多数机器人学专家，对人形机器人的工业应用比它们承担家务更感兴趣。（英伟达（Nvidia）负责物理人工智能仿真的莱芭雷迪安博士（Rev Lebaredian）说过：‘你先会在麦当劳看到它们。’）Apptronik 的阿波罗（Apollo）人形机器人——瓷白色机身、节肢状的肢端和粗壮结实的腿部——已经在梅赛德斯—奔驰（Mercedes-Benz）旗下的欧洲汽车工厂投入生产使用。就目前而言，这种机器人只限于挑选零件和装载传送带等工作。完成这些任务需要它有相当的力量，这也意味着一旦发生故障就可能伤到人。因此，这些机器人都被与其他工人隔离开来。

paragraphs.en: Humanoids make up only a tiny fraction of the five million or so robots currently deployed in factories worldwide. Mike Haley, a senior researcher at Autodesk, a maker of industrial-design software, has spent much of his career programming these specialized, non-humanoid factory bots. He told me that he has never seen a humanoid do anything useful in an industrial setting. Simpler robotics solutions, like detached arms for spraying paint, or autonomous forklifts for moving pallets, are more efficient, cost less, have fewer moving parts, and require less maintenance. “I truly believe that we have a moment a few years down the road from now where we look back at this humanoid thing and go, ‘Boy, that was bloody stupid,’ ” Haley said.
paragraphs.zh: 在全球工厂目前部署的约五百万台机器人中，人形机器人只占极小一部分。迈克·海利是Autodesk（一家工业设计软件制造商）的高级研究员，他职业生涯的大部分时间都用来编程这些专用的非人形工厂机器人。他告诉我，他从未见过有人形机器人在工业环境里做出任何有用的事情。像喷漆用的独立机械臂，或用于移动托盘的无人叉车这类更简单的机器人方案更高效、成本更低、活动部件更少，也更容易维护。海利说：“我真心相信，几年后我们回头看这波人形浪潮时，会说：‘当时真是太蠢了。’”

paragraphs.en: Commercial service robots are already commonplace in China, but these are cheap, special-purpose devices that deliver food or fold laundry. Expensive humanoids may not be needed for these tasks. At Skild, Pathak’s solution is to isolate a robot’s parts to fit the task at hand. “Let’s say you’re putting a robot in a factory like this, and you’re assembling something—should you really spend energy on the legs?” he asked. “I can separate the torso and put it on the table, then use the legs to carry something else.”
paragraphs.zh: 商业服务机器人在中国已经很常见，但这些只是廉价、用途单一的设备，用来送餐或叠衣物。对于这些任务来说，昂贵的人形机器人可能并不需要。Skild的Pathak认为，解决方案是按任务拆分机器人的部件。“假设你把一个机器人放进这样的工厂里，而且它要组装某个东西——你真的要把精力花在它的腿上吗？”他问道。“我可以把躯干分离出来放在桌子上，然后用它的腿去搬别的东西。”

paragraphs.en: It seems that 1X is taking the Apple approach, designing beautiful products from scratch and controlling the entire manufacturing process. Skild is taking the Android approach, writing cross-platform software and running it on hardware made by other companies. Apptronik, Unitree, Tesla, and others—maybe they will be like Nokia, or Blackberry. Or maybe one of them will win.
paragraphs.zh: 看起来1X正在走苹果路线，从零开始打造漂亮的产品并控制整个制造流程。Skild则是走安卓路线，在其他公司制造的硬件上运行跨平台软件。Apptronik、Unitree、Tesla等其他公司——也许它们会像诺基亚或黑莓那样。或者，也许其中之一会赢。

paragraphs.en: About seven or eight million years ago, a branch of primates broke off and started developing superior brains, dexterous hands, and the capacity for language. That branch diversified into Australopithecus, and Ardipithecus, and Homo erectus. Eventually, Homo sapiens emerged and took over. Surveying the diversity of humanoid forms at this early stage of robotics, I could see something similar occurring. The essential form—two arms, two legs, a torso, and a head—was consistent, but there were many variations. Agility’s Digit has a square head and inverted kneecaps, like a grasshopper. Apptronik’s Apollo uses a classic sci-fi design, with digital readouts on its mouth and chest. Boston Dynamics’s Atlas has a toylike carapace and a glowing, hollow circle for a head. Tesla’s Optimus, Neura’s 4NE-1, and Figure’s 03 have all converged on similarly anonymous and ever so slightly sinister designs: white bodies with smooth black faceplates. One of these is likely the evolutionary ancestor of all that is to come.
paragraphs.zh: 大约七八百万年前，灵长类中的一个分支分化出来，开始发展出更高级的大脑、灵巧的手和语言能力。这个分支进一步演化出了南方古猿、阿迪普斯猿和直立人。最终，智人出现并占据主导地位。回望当前人形机器人的形态多样性，我看到了一种相似的发展：核心形态——两只手臂、两条腿、一个躯干和一个头部——始终一致，但差异却很多。Agility 的 Digit 头部是方形的，膝盖是倒向内侧的，像蝗虫。Apptronik 的 Apollo 则采用经典科幻风格设计，嘴部和胸前都有数字化读数。波士顿动力的Atlas 有一个像玩具一样的外壳，头部是一圈发光的空心圆。Tesla 的 Optimus、Neura 的 4NE-1 以及 Figure 的 03 都逐渐汇聚到相似且略带阴森感的外形：白色机身配光滑的黑色面板。这些中的某一种，很可能是未来一切形态的演化祖先。

paragraphs.en: The missing link might be the hand. The human hand is one of three pinnacles of hominid evolution, along with the brain and the voice box. It is by far the most capable manipulator in the animal kingdom, and can perform twenty-seven independent motions. Robotic hands lag far behind: we are years away from a robot that can both tie its shoes and shuffle a deck of cards.
paragraphs.zh: 缺失的一环可能是“手”。人手与大脑、声带一起，被认为是灵长目进化中的三大顶点之一。它是动物界里最强大的操控工具之一，能够完成二十七种独立的运动。机器人之手则远远落后于此：要等上许多年，我们才可能拥有一台既能系鞋带又能洗牌的机器人。

paragraphs.en: At 1X, I saw rows of hands and forearms, denuded of housing and grasping toward the ceiling. Technicians seated along an assembly line were fastening artificial tendons to the fingers to link them with actuators in the wrist. Others attached electrical wire to sensors on the fingertips; the wire would eventually be routed to the robot’s brain. Nearby, a finger was being tested for durability. It pointed, curled back, then repeated the movements. A counter on a monitor noted that this action had been performed 2,860,631 times.
paragraphs.zh: 在 1X，我看到一排排手和前臂，已经去除了外壳，向上伸展。沿着装配线坐着的技术人员正在把人工肌腱固定到手指上，以便将它们与手腕里的执行器连接。另一些人则把电线接到指尖的传感器上；这些电线最终会被接入机器人的“大脑”。旁边，一根手指正在耐久性测试中：它先指向、再向后弯，再重复这些动作。显示器上的计数器显示，这个动作已经执行了 2,860,631 次。

paragraphs.en: But here, again, the mechanical engineering was far ahead of the A.I. This point was confirmed during a demonstration at Skild, where I watched a Unitree robot carefully grab a white ceramic coffee cup from a table and set it upright in a bin. I moved the coffee cup back to the tabletop, and the robot’s eyelike cameras seemed to follow. Then I turned the cup on its side. The robot seemed perplexed. It moved its hand about six inches from the cup and began to grab uselessly in the air.
paragraphs.zh: 但在这里，机械工程再次远远领先于人工智能。这一点在 Skild 的一次演示中得到了进一步印证，我看到一台 Unitree 机器人小心地从桌上拿起一个白色陶瓷咖啡杯，并把它竖直放进一个箱子。之后我把咖啡杯放回桌面，机器人的眼状摄像头似乎在跟随它。接着我把杯子侧着放。机器人显得很困惑。它把手移到离杯子约六英寸的位置，然后在空中徒劳地开始抓取。

paragraphs.en: With a disapproving look, a technician set the cup upright, and the robot immediately returned it to the container. I took a blue plastic bowl from the bin and placed it upside down. The robot identified the object but couldn’t get a grip on it. As its hand pushed the bowl around fruitlessly, I gave a little smirk of human superiority. But then the robot stopped. And—I swear to this—it started to think. After about ten seconds, it perceived that the overturned bowl had a raised circular foot. Pinching the foot of the bowl, the robot picked it up and placed it in the bin. “See?” the technician said. “It figured it out.” ♦
paragraphs.zh: 一位技术员不满地看了它一眼，把杯子重新摆正，机器人立刻又把它放回容器。我从箱子里拿出一个蓝色塑料碗并倒扣放上去。机器人识别出这个物体，但抓不牢。它的手只是在空中无效地推着碗，我因此暗自露出了一点“人类高高在上”的得意笑。可是它停了下来。然后——我发誓——它开始思考了。大约十秒后，它察觉到倒扣的碗有一个凸起的圆形底脚。机器人捏住碗脚把它提了起来，然后放回箱子。技术员说：“看到了吗？它想明白了。” ♦
